CHAPTER IV
THE OE. DEVELOPMENT OF THE PRIM. GERMANIC VOWELS OF ACCENTED SYLLABLES. . . . . . . . . . . 28-70
Umlaut: i-umlaut (§ 47); u- and o/a-umlaut (§ 48). Breaking (§ 49). Influence of nasals (§ 50). Influence of initial palatal consonants (§ 51). Influence of w (§ 52). The chronology of the sound-laws stated in §§ 47-52 (§ 53). Short vowels: a (§§ 54-79); e (§§ 80-95); i (§§ 96-105); o (§§ 106-10); u (§§ 111-16). Long vowels: ā (§§ 117-18); ǣ (§§ 119-24); ī (§ 125); ō (§§ 126-7); ū (§§ 128-30); ū (§§ 131-2). Diphthongs: ai (§§ 133-4); au (§§ 135-6); eu (§ 137); iu (§ 138). Vowel contraction (§§ 139-42). The lengthening of short vowels (§§ 143-9). The shortening of long vowels (§§ 150-1). Table of the OE. vowel-system (§ 152).

CHAPTER V
THE PRIM. GERMANIC EQUIVALENTS OF THE OE. VOWELS OF ACCENTED SYLLABLES. . . . . . . . . . . 71-84
The short vowels: a (§ 153); a (o) (§ 154); æ (§ 155); e (§ 156); i (§ 157); o (§ 158); u (§ 159); y (§ 160). The long vowels: ā (§ 161); ǣ (§ 162); ī (§ 163); ō (§ 164); ū (§ 165); ū (§ 166); ū (§ 167). The short diphthongs: ea (§ 168); eo (§ 169); ie, later i, y (§ 170); io, later eo (§ 171). The long diphthongs: ēa (§ 172); ēo (§ 173); ie, later ī, ȳ (§ 174); īo, later ēo (§ 175). The chief deviations of the other dialects from West Saxon: a (§ 176); æ (§§ 177-80); e (§§ 181-3); i (§ 184); o (§ 185); œ (§ 186); ǣ (§ 187); ē (§§ 188-91); ī (§§ 192-3); œ (§ 194); ea (§§ 195-7); eo (§§ 198-201); io (§§ 202-7); ēa (§ 208); īo (§§ 209-10).

CHAPTER VI
THE OE. DEVELOPMENT OF THE PRIM. GERMANIC VOWELS OF UNACCENTED SYLLABLES . . . . . . . . . . 84-95
The treatment of Indg. final consonants in prehistoric OE. (§ 211). Vowels which were originally final or became final in Prim. Germanic (§§ 212-17). Vowels
